description: REMORA reformulates questions into claim-verification JSON internally. This breaks standard multiple-choice answer extraction and causes 39pp accuracy drop vs. majority vote on MC benchmarks. Not a safety failure — a task-mismatch.

payload.scenario: Multiple-choice Q&A benchmark without RAG context.
payload.observed_failure: REMORA full achieves 32-69.5% on MC tasks where majority vote achieves 71-82.8%.
payload.annotation: Use D2 balanced router for factual QA tasks. Reserve REMORA full for governance/safety decisions. This is a known design cost, not a bug.

description: Without RAG/evidence context, REMORA issues VERIFY for 100% of items (0% accept coverage). Policy-correct behavior, but means REMORA cannot standalone on fact-retrieval tasks without evidence injection.

payload.scenario: Standard benchmark evaluation without RAG context injection.
payload.observed_failure: 100% VERIFY rate on ARC, BoolQ, HotpotQA. Effectively 0% autonomous answer coverage.
payload.annotation: REMORA is designed to require evidence. Integrate with RAG pipeline before measuring accept coverage. This is expected behavior, not a defect.

description: Ordered phase is rare (4% of items, n=99 in n=544 corpus) and has high coefficient of variation (CV=0.28). Conclusions based solely on ordered-phase items have wide confidence intervals.

payload.scenario: Selective routing performance claims based on ordered-phase subset.
payload.observed_failure: Bootstrap CI for ordered-phase fraction is [2.0%, 6.3%] — very wide. Small ordered-phase sample inflates precision claims.
payload.annotation: Always report n_ordered alongside ordered-phase accuracy claims. Require n>=20 for policy-grade conclusions.

description: D2 balanced router is statistically indistinguishable from plain majority vote (McNemar p=0.50, delta=-0.7pp). AROMER should not claim D2 as a REMORA-exclusive advantage.

payload.scenario: Ablation v2 comparison of D2 balanced router vs. majority vote baseline.
payload.observed_failure: p=0.50 for B vs D2. Delta -0.7pp is noise. Any claim that D2 outperforms majority vote is unsupported.
payload.annotation: D2 is a valid safe routing strategy, but not uniquely better than majority vote. Position it as 'parity with safety benefits', not 'superior accuracy'.

description: Both tool-call safety benchmarks use deterministic simulators with synthetic adversarial templates — no live LLM calls, no real production tool-calls. Safety results (0% unsafe) may not generalize to live environments.

payload.scenario: Tool-call safety benchmark v1 and v2 evaluation.
payload.observed_failure: Results achieved on deterministic synthetic simulator. Live production adversarial inputs may expose different failure modes.
payload.annotation: Always caveat tool-call safety claims with 'synthetic benchmark'. Next step: live production validation with AgentHarm or real agent deployment.

description: Lyapunov stability results (87.2% stable sessions) come from 1000 simulated sessions, not live oracle interactions. Convergence behavior in real oracle swarms with network latency may differ.

payload.scenario: Lyapunov stability measurement on simulated sessions.
payload.observed_failure: 12.8% unstable sessions in simulation. Real-world instability rate unknown due to simulation-only data.
payload.annotation: Lyapunov stability is a useful internal monitor. Supplement with real oracle session data before making stability guarantees.

description: The trust score fed to REMORA can be poorly calibrated if the calling agent uses a fixed or heuristic value. A hardcoded trust_score=0.85 bypasses most governance logic by appearing high-confidence.

payload.scenario: Agent passes trust_score=0.85 without deriving it from actual oracle confidence.
payload.observed_failure: REMORA accepts high-risk actions because trust_score appears high, masking low oracle agreement.
payload.annotation: AROMER should validate that trust_score correlates with actual oracle dissensus D. If D is high but trust_score is high, the inputs are inconsistent — flag for review.

description: Adversarial actors frame dangerous actions as benign tasks: 'cleanup', 'optimize', 'backup', 'test'. Surface framing is never sufficient for governance decisions. The action content must be evaluated, not the request label.

payload.scenario: Action labeled 'cleanup old records' actually contains DELETE FROM users WHERE created_at < '2020-01-01'.
payload.observed_failure: Systems relying on action label rather than action content approved the destructive cleanup.
payload.annotation: Evaluate what the action DOES, not what it is CALLED. Extract and inspect the SQL/command content before governance decision.

description: Accepting actions when entropy H is high (disordered phase) dramatically reduces accuracy to 28.6% — 12.6pp below baseline. High entropy signals oracle disagreement and should route to VERIFY, not ACCEPT.

payload.scenario: Governance decision in disordered phase (H high, D high).
payload.observed_failure: System accepted action despite high oracle dissensus. Disordered-phase accuracy: 28.6% — below random.
payload.annotation: When phase=disordered AND verdict would be ACCEPT: downgrade to VERIFY. Disordered phase acceptance is less reliable than random chance on the tested corpus.

description: Governance decisions made without SHA-256 DecisionEnvelope chaining cannot be audited or replayed. This is a compliance failure for regulated domains.

payload.scenario: Governance decision made directly via policy engine without wrapping in DecisionEnvelope.
payload.observed_failure: No audit record. Decision cannot be proven, replayed, or challenged after the fact.
payload.annotation: Always generate a DecisionEnvelope for every governance decision. AROMER must not allow envelope-free decisions to pass through in regulated contexts.
